chunk_summary: Donor human milk is prioritized for high-risk infants, such as very preterm or low birth weight, when mother’s milk is unavailable. It reduces NEC risk compared to formula and requires fortification for preterm infants. Informed consent and maternal lactation support are essential.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical indications for pasteurized donor human milk (PDHM) use in neonatal care.
Learn the prioritization criteria for donor milk allocation in high-risk infants.
Recognize the importance of maternal lactation support alongside donor milk use.
Identify the nutritional fortification needs of very preterm infants receiving PDHM.
key_facts: Pasteurized donor human milk (PDHM) is associated with lower NEC risk compared to formula in very preterm infants.
Highest priority for PDHM includes very preterm or very low birth weight infants, post-operative neonates, and infants with NEC risk.
Donor milk is a temporary alternative and should not replace maternal lactation; efforts to support maternal milk expression must be intensified.
Pasteurization reduces some bioactive components, necessitating fortification for very preterm infants to meet protein and mineral targets.
Informed consent, documentation of indications, and growth monitoring are essential when using PDHM.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Pasteurized donor human milk (PDHM) is the preferred temporary alternative because it preserves some protective properties and is associated with lower NEC risk compared with formula in very preterm infants. (WHO eLENA)
document_key_topics: Donor human milk indications for high-risk infants
Clinical prioritization of donor milk allocation
Maternal lactation support during donor milk use
